author: @k.chriscaldwell4141
text:
Not condoning posting fake videos, but a little good does comes of it: People that don't know better, or should, do get some experience constructing something and testing it. There are worse things in the world. It will fail. But we do learn more from our failures than our successes.

At around 12-years-old, a friend and I got hold of a couple of Big Wheel tire and axle sets. We built a crude unpowered go-cart chassis for them. We had measured the width of the axles from the outside of each tire, not the inside. The axles would not fit the finished chassis. Doh!

Much was learned, though: Experience, new skills, and life lessons.

author: @patglenn7762
text:
Actually, the CD thing will work - but NOT because it's a solar cell. The aluminum layer of the CD works together with the copper wire to form a bi-metal battery. Putting it in the sun adds infrared (heat) to the equation, speeding up the reaction. So it's not actually converting light to electricity - but heat. This principle will work with any dis-similar metals. Nothing more than ions transferring from one metal to the other. The reaction will continue until one of the layers runs out of ions and eventually corrodes. 

Ever seen one of those battery-less fans that you place upon the fireplace register? Light the fire and in about 10 - 15 minutes the fan is whirring away. Same principle.

author: @nickopeters
text:
I didn't know about those videos, although I definitely had seen, many videos claiming to be able to do various kinds of "electricity" tricks by connecting wires to various objects that obviously had had no such capability.  

Aluminium is the next-best, electrical-conductor after copper (silver, had been the very-best--;) it also can be polished to make a great mirror, or even the vaguely laser-like or hologram-like effects seen on CD's, but that still doesn't mean that it was transforming the light into luminescent, electromagnetic "plasma" beams or fields off of the surface of the CD--; otherwise reminiscent of the way the solar wind interacts with the earth's magnetic poles to create the "auroras--.". 

The most obvious giveaway that these could never form "solar" cells--; is the fact that they are expressly-designed to "reflect" light "away--;" the opposite of the first thing any "solar" cell must do--; namely to "absorb" the light so that next it could be "converted into" whatever form of electricity--.  

That in fact is probably at least pretty-close to what the CD "reader" in fact already would have to "do--:". Namely, to take the rapidly-sequenced light "pulses" reflected by the prisms, from the laser-beam, and to convert "them" into the electronic energy "equivalent--;" analogous to what a guitar-pickup would do with the mechanical movement of the nearest segment of a vibrating, ferrous wire (string--;).  

Thanks for letting me know about this annoying, clickbait con, anyway--.

author: @deslomeslager
text:
About cd .. .. If you have a CD player and do no longer need it, you can take out the ejection system. Thing is: if you put an LED (or two in reverse) on the motor and operate the eject mechanism (open, close, open, close), the leds will light up. And yes, there is another motor in the middle, but you will need to spin it a bit faster. The nice thing of the eject system is that it has gearing in it (See Rob's previous video about making your own, 1 to 128 ration).
The CDrom can be used to create a capacitor, not very spectacular.
The best use I know of for a CD is in the garden, hanging (dangling) from a rope, to scare birds away.

replies.author: @timhartherz5652
replies.text:
I worked at a company which build the machines to manufacture CD's/DVD's neither the ordinary nor write/rewritable ones contain silicone.
They use a thin layer of metal which is been sputtered on as a reflective layer as well as a light-sensitive dye for the (re)writeable ones.
We experimeted with all kinds of metals for the reflective layer though, copper gold platinum you name it we tried it, silicone was none of them though.
But Aluminium was the best cost/reflectivness compromise.

replies.author: @Kangsteri
replies.text: @scotttovey Yes :D I'm currently studying if it might be possible to make portable AM rectenna to power 1,5v FM / Mp3 player... Biggest issue seems to be sourcing genuine low voltage UHF/VHF/RF diodes for cheap. Those good old germanium ones are nearly 1€ a peace + shipping. The CD actually might work well and be better with "spider coil" configuration. But I'm also going to use some LC magic, so I will try some dark green or dark grey (Mn-Zn) toroids to get more power.
